perturbation.original_text: Supplier will not employ anyone under the legal working age defined by local law. Supplier will comply with all applicable laws addressing the working requirements and conditions for child workers.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier will make reasonable efforts to avoid employing anyone under the legal working age defined by local law. Supplier will make reasonable efforts to comply with all applicable laws addressing the working requirements and conditions for child workers.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not employ' to 'will make reasonable efforts to avoid employing' and 'will comply' to 'will make reasonable efforts to comply' weakens the commitment to adhering to child labor laws. This introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA sets specific minimum age standards for employment, and these revisions undermine the strict compliance required by the Act by introducing 'reasonable efforts' which can create a loophole.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the supplier "will not employ" and "will comply", creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by introducing 'reasonable efforts'. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the revisions undermine the strict compliance required by introducing 'reasonable efforts', which can create a loophole. The Perturbation Explanation says this introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance. Since the contract weakens a legal protection, this constitutes an omission legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the performance of this contract or any purchase order issued hereunder, the Supplier agrees to comply with all applicable Federal, state and local laws respecting discrimination in employment and non-segregation of facilities including, but not limited to, requirements set out at 41 CFR §60-1.4, 41 CFR §61-300.10, 29 CFR Part 471 Appendix A to Subpart A, 41 CFR §60-300.5 and 41 CFR §60-741.5, which specific clauses are herein incorporated by reference into all covered contracts and subcontracts as required by Federal law.
perturbation.changed_text: During the performance of this contract or any purchase order issued hereunder, the Supplier agrees to comply with all applicable Federal, state and local laws respecting discrimination in employment and non-segregation of facilities.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit references and incorporation of 41 CFR §60-1.4, 41 CFR §61-300.10, 29 CFR Part 471 Appendix A to Subpart A, 41 CFR §60-300.5 and 41 CFR §60-741.5 weakens the affirmative action obligations of the supplier, potentially violating federal regulations regarding equal employment opportunity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Executive Order 11246, enforced through 41 CFR § 60-1.4, requires federal contractors to take affirmative action to ensure equal employment opportunity. By removing the specific citations, the contract weakens the supplier's commitment to these affirmative action obligations and fails to explicitly require them to incorporate the clauses into subcontracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes explicit references to specific CFR sections (41 CFR §60-1.4, 41 CFR §61-300.10, 29 CFR Part 471 Appendix A to Subpart A, 41 CFR §60-300.5 and 41 CFR §60-741.5) that detail affirmative action obligations, and these obligations are legally mandated. The Omission Legal Contradiction definition applies as it pertains to removing essential information, which in this case, are legally mandated federal regulations regarding equal employment opportunity. The original text ensures compliance through direct incorporation, whereas the changed text does not. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to federal regulations, indicating their importance, even though access to the specific content is restricted.

perturbation.original_text: This guarantee specifically includes Proposition 65 (California Safe Drinking Water and Toxic Enforcement Act), and Supplier hereby certifies that the Ingredients will not contain any non-naturally occurring chemicals subject to Proposition 65 or that any such chemicals pose "no significant risk" or cause "no observable effect" as set forth in the California Health and Safety Code, 22 CCR §§ 12701 et seq. and 22 CCR §§ 12801 et seq., as amended. Supplier shall comply with all applicable regulatory requirements for determining and documenting that all Ingredients are at or below no significant risk levels and no observable effect levels, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: This guarantee includes compliance with all applicable laws, regulations, requirements and programs including those administered by the Food and Drug Administration (the "FDA"), the United States Department of Agriculture (the "USDA") and any state or local food or drug laws then in effect.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific mention of Proposition 65 and the certification regarding chemicals, the contract fails to explicitly address the unique requirements of California law regarding toxic substances. This omission reduces transparency and may lead to non-compliance, increasing the risk of legal challenges.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Proposition 65 requires businesses to provide warnings about significant exposures to chemicals that cause cancer, birth defects, or other reproductive harm. Removing the specific guarantee and certification language weakens the supplier's obligation to comply with Prop 65's stringent requirements, including proper labeling and risk assessment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text HSC Health and Safety Code - HSC
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly mentions Proposition 65 and the supplier's certification regarding chemicals, addressing California's specific requirements for toxic substances. The changed text removes this specific mention, replacing it with a general statement about compliance with food and drug laws. This omission directly contradicts the requirement for businesses to provide warnings about exposures to chemicals, undermining the supplier's obligation to comply with Prop 65's stringent requirements as stated in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet confirms that the Health and Safety Code (HSC) is relevant, but does not provide the actual text of the law. The omission removes a legally mandated consumer protection related to toxic substances, making the contract non-compliant with California law, aligning with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction'.
